title: 38 days of nothing: what actually happened to my github
content:
if you've looked at my github profile in the last month you've seen a flat line. no green squares. just the gray grid of someone who apparently stopped existing as a developer around mid-may.the word burnout gets used loosely so i want to be specific. it wasn't 'i'm exhausted and can't function.' it was quieter. i could open the editor, i just didn't want to. everything felt marginally harder than it should. the activation cost of starting anything was slightly too high, every single time.

// the timeline

[may 15] last commit. nothing dramatic. pushed some work, closed the laptop. didn't know it'd be the last one for 38 days.[may 16-18] needed rest. actually resting. didn't open the editor. felt fine about it. this part was correct.[may 19-23] friction without reason. would open a project, stare at it, close it. had ideas, zero activation energy to execute any of them. this is where the burnout was actually sitting.
[may 24-31] life happened. real-world overhead that doesn't get committed. home stuff. people stuff. sleep debt. not interesting things to blog about, just true ones. also used it as cover for continued avoidance. worth noting. [jun 1-9] streak anxiety loop. gap got long enough that returning felt disproportionately significant. started thinking 'when i come back it should be with something good.' didn't have something good. waited. gap grew. a specific, stupid psychological trap.[jun 10-17] working, just not here. building things again locally. nothing pushed. half-formed ideas, experiments, stuff not ready to be public. the work existed. git didn't know about it. [jun 18-21] looked at the profile properly. that flat gray stretch is something. decided to write about it instead of quietly resuming. [jun 22] this post. first commit since may 15. the phases [1] needed rest. actually resting. // fine first few days you stop, you're just tired. healthy and correct. the mistake is thinking this is where burnout ends. [2] friction without reason. // concerning rested but still not moving. you sit down and nothing happens. most people don't recognize this as burnout because the tiredness is gone but the drive hasn't come back. you feel fine but you're producing nothing. [3] life as an excuse. // neutral
real things needed handling. but i noticed i was relieved to have a reason not to sit at the desk. the external stuff gave cover for what was really just continued avoidance. [4] streak anxiety. // the stupid one once the gap is long enough, returning feels like a Statement. you wait until you have something good. you don't have something good. you wait more. the gap grows. repeat. [5] quiet re-entry. // recovery started building again locally, no pressure of it being public. the graph didn't know, but i did. this is where things started feeling normal again. the graph problem

the github contribution graph measures commits to public repositories. that's it. it doesn't measure how much you're thinking, reading, designing, or building locally. it doesn't measure the two hours you spent understanding something under the hood. it doesn't measure the stuff you decided not to ship because it wasn't ready. a gray graph can mean you stopped building. it can also mean you were going through something, or working privately, or just living your life. the graph can't tell the difference and neither can anyone looking at your profile. the version of yourself that builds for public validation will always be fighting burnout. the version that builds because the problem is interesting will have long gaps on github and be fine with it. i'm trying to be the second one. i don't always manage it. coming back not setting a streak goal. not committing to posting every day. the lesson isn't 'be more consistent', it's 'notice sooner when you've drifted from building because it's interesting into building because you feel like you should be seen building. the things i want to work on are still there. the drive came back on its own timeline, not mine. that's how it works. if you've had a gap in your own output, this isn't a comeback post, this is just a wednesday. the gap doesn't need a narrative. but if writing about it helps someone feel less weird about their own flat graph, then the commit was worth it. git commit -m "docs: explain the silence"

title: Welcome to the New Site
content:
I've completely overhauled the website to bring it back to the golden age of computing.

Enjoy the crisp gray backgrounds, the comforting teal desktop, and the satisfying click of un-smoothed fonts.

This new design is cleaner, faster, and more "Windows 95" than ever before.

Also open the developer console and type "bsod()" to see a system integrity violation.

title: Happy New Year!
content: I hope you all had a wonderful holiday break. Sorry for not posting in December, I was away on holiday. I’m excited to be back and can’t wait to see what this year brings for technology!

title: Osaka '09
content: Recently I have been working on my retro Linux Distro, Osaka '09. Development is frustrating...

title: UNC2891 Hackers Use Linux Malware in Major Banking Security Heists
content:
UNC2891 slipped Raspberry Pi implants into ATM network paths, used simple Linux privilege-escalation tools, and hid their activity with bind mounts to quietly control transaction switches for years.

That foothold let them clone cards, run mule cashouts across multiple countries, and exploit long-standing structural weaknesses in banking networks that looked secure only on paper.

title: Offline LLMs Are Actually Useful Now
content:
Quantization, lightweight runtimes, and model distillation let multi-billion-parameter models run locally on laptops.

For privacy-first workflows and reproducible experiments, an offline model can be dramatically more practical than it was two years ago, that is if you accept some tradeoffs in latency and accuracy.

title: CLI over GUI
content:
Terminals outperform GUIs for anyone who knows their tools. Commands chain, automate, and scale in ways clicking never will.

A GUI shows options; a CLI lets you shape the system.
